comment_text.sn22.56:1.1: The aggregates lack a formal “analysis” discourse. This discourse and the next, however, fill that gap by defining the aggregates. The definitions of form, feeling, and consciousness are shared with dependent origination (SN 12.2).
comment_text.sn22.56:1.5: Compare the “three rounds” of SN 56.11:9.1 and the “eight rounds” of AN 8.64:10.1.
comment_text.sn22.56:3.2: The “principal states” (mahābhūtā) are earth, water, fire, and air, corresponding to the states of matter, namely solid, liquid, plasma, and gas. For the history of this term, see note on MN 28:4.2. | “Derived” (upādāya) form is not explained in the suttas, but one passage indicates it includes “space” (MN 28:26.1). The Abhidhamma traditions explain it as including the objects of the senses, the subtle matter that receives sense impressions, and various other material phenomena.
comment_text.sn22.56:3.4: For the topic of “fuel” (āhāra), here primarily meaning “food”, see  SN 12.11, SN 12.63, MN 9:10.2, SN 47.42.
comment_text.sn22.56:4.2: Those who “have a footing” (gādhanti) are the noble individuals who are trainees. A gādha is a “ford” across a river (Rig Veda 7.60.7, 7.97.8, 10.106.9; Gopatha Brāhmaṇa 1.5.2a), a safe path on which one has a “footing” to cross the water.
comment_text.sn22.56:5.2: Kevala has the root meaning of “entire”, “exclusive”. For the Jains, kevalī came to mean “one whose knowledge is entire”, i.e. an omniscient one. The Buddha used it to indicate one who has consummated the entire path.
comment_text.sn22.56:10.1: Here “choices” (saṅkhārā) are explicitly defined as “intentions” (sañcetanā), a meaning that is consistent in this context and reflected in the common idiom “choices and intentions” (eg. SN 12.37:1.3). See notes on SN 12.2:13.3 and SN 12.51:9.1. | This was maintained in the early Abhidhamma Vibhaṅga (Vb 1), but in the later systematization of the Dhammasaṅgaṇī, the saṅkhārākhandha_ was extended to include all mental factors except feeling, perception, and consciousness (Ds 2.1.1:75.1). As an umbrella category it was then fully defined in the commentarial list of fifty mental factors, of which intention is only one (see Visuddhimagga 14.131). The aim of the Abhidhamma project is to be fully inclusive and systematic, but the role of this aggregate in the Suttas is not to be inclusive, but rather to highlight the significance of intention, will, or volition in creating and maintaining our sense of self.
comment_text.sn22.56:15.3: For the phrase “no cycle of rebirths to be found” (vaṭṭaṁ tesaṁ natthi paññāpanāya), compare DN 15:22.6.

root_text.sn22.56:1.5: Yāvakīvañcāhaṁ, bhikkhave, ime pañcupādānakkhandhe catuparivaṭṭaṁ yathābhūtaṁ nābbhaññāsiṁ, neva tāvāhaṁ, bhikkhave, sadevake loke samārake sabrahmake sassamaṇabrāhmaṇiyā pajāya sadevamanussāya anuttaraṁ sammāsambodhiṁ abhisambuddhoti paccaññāsiṁ.
root_text.sn22.56:1.6: Yato ca khvāhaṁ, bhikkhave, ime pañcupādānakkhandhe catuparivaṭṭaṁ yathābhūtaṁ abbhaññāsiṁ, athāhaṁ, bhikkhave, sadevake loke …pe…
root_text.sn22.56:2.2: Rūpaṁ abbhaññāsiṁ, rūpasamudayaṁ abbhaññāsiṁ, rūpanirodhaṁ abbhaññāsiṁ, rūpanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ abbhaññāsiṁ;
root_text.sn22.56:2.6: viññāṇaṁ abbhaññāsiṁ, viññāṇasamudayaṁ abbhaññāsiṁ, viññāṇanirodhaṁ abbhaññāsiṁ, viññāṇanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ abbhaññāsiṁ.
root_text.sn22.56:3.2: Cattāro ca mahābhūtā catunnañca mahābhūtānaṁ upādāya rūpaṁ.
root_text.sn22.56:3.6: Ayameva ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo rūpanirodhagāminī paṭipadā, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.sn22.56:4.1: Ye hi keci, bhikkhave, samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā evaṁ rūpaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ rūpasamudayaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ rūpanirodhaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ rūpanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ abhiññāya rūpassa nibbidāya virāgāya nirodhāya paṭipannā, te suppaṭipannā.
root_text.sn22.56:4.2: Ye suppaṭipannā, te imasmiṁ dhammavinaye gādhanti.
root_text.sn22.56:5.1: Ye ca kho keci, bhikkhave, samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā evaṁ rūpaṁ abhiññāya …pe… evaṁ rūpanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ abhiññāya, rūpassa nibbidā virāgā nirodhā anupādā vimuttā te suvimuttā.
root_text.sn22.56:5.3: Ye kevalino vaṭṭaṁ tesaṁ natthi paññāpanāya.
root_text.sn22.56:6.3: cakkhusamphassajā vedanā, sotasamphassajā vedanā, ghānasamphassajā vedanā, jivhāsamphassajā vedanā, kāyasamphassajā vedanā, manosamphassajā vedanā.
root_text.sn22.56:6.7: Ayameva ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo vedanānirodhagāminī paṭipadā, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.sn22.56:7.1: Ye hi keci, bhikkhave, samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā evaṁ vedanaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ vedanāsamudayaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ vedanānirodhaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ vedanānirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ abhiññāya vedanāya nibbidāya virāgāya nirodhāya paṭipannā, te suppaṭipannā.
root_text.sn22.56:7.2: Ye suppaṭipannā, te imasmiṁ dhammavinaye gādhanti.
root_text.sn22.56:8.1: Ye ca kho keci, bhikkhave, samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā evaṁ vedanaṁ abhiññāya …pe… evaṁ vedanānirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ abhiññāya …pe…
root_text.sn22.56:9.3: rūpasaññā, saddasaññā, gandhasaññā, rasasaññā, phoṭṭhabbasaññā, dhammasaññā.
root_text.sn22.56:9.7: Ayameva ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo saññānirodhagāminī paṭipadā, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.sn22.56:10.3: rūpasañcetanā, saddasañcetanā, gandhasañcetanā, rasasañcetanā, phoṭṭhabbasañcetanā, dhammasañcetanā.
root_text.sn22.56:10.7: Ayameva ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo saṅkhāranirodhagāminī paṭipadā, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.sn22.56:11.1: Ye hi keci, bhikkhave, samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā evaṁ saṅkhāre abhiññāya, evaṁ saṅkhārasamudayaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ saṅkhāranirodhaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ saṅkhāranirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ abhiññāya saṅkhārānaṁ nibbidāya virāgāya nirodhāya paṭipannā, te suppaṭipannā.
root_text.sn22.56:11.2: Ye suppaṭipannā, te imasmiṁ dhammavinaye gādhanti.
root_text.sn22.56:12.1: Ye ca kho keci, bhikkhave, samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā evaṁ saṅkhāre abhiññāya, evaṁ saṅkhārasamudayaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ saṅkhāranirodhaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ saṅkhāranirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ abhiññāya saṅkhārānaṁ nibbidā virāgā nirodhā anupādā vimuttā, te suvimuttā.
root_text.sn22.56:12.3: Ye kevalino vaṭṭaṁ tesaṁ natthi paññāpanāya.
root_text.sn22.56:13.3: cakkhuviññāṇaṁ, sotaviññāṇaṁ, ghānaviññāṇaṁ, jivhāviññāṇaṁ, kāyaviññāṇaṁ, manoviññāṇaṁ.
root_text.sn22.56:13.7: Ayameva ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo viññāṇanirodhagāminī paṭipadā, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.sn22.56:14.1: Ye hi keci, bhikkhave, samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā evaṁ viññāṇaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ viññāṇasamudayaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ viññāṇanirodhaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ viññāṇanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ abhiññāya viññāṇassa nibbidāya virāgāya nirodhāya paṭipannā, te suppaṭipannā.
root_text.sn22.56:14.2: Ye suppaṭipannā, te imasmiṁ dhammavinaye gādhanti.
root_text.sn22.56:15.1: Ye ca kho keci, bhikkhave, samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā evaṁ viññāṇaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ viññāṇasamudayaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ viññāṇanirodhaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ viññāṇanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ abhiññāya viññāṇassa nibbidā virāgā nirodhā anupādā vimuttā, te suvimuttā.
root_text.sn22.56:15.3: Ye kevalino vaṭṭaṁ tesaṁ natthi paññāpanāyā”ti.

translation_text.sn22.56:1.2: “Mendicants, there are these five grasping aggregates.
translation_text.sn22.56:1.4: The grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness.
translation_text.sn22.56:1.5: As long as I didn’t truly understand these five grasping aggregates in four rounds, I didn’t announce my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans.
translation_text.sn22.56:1.6: But when I did truly understand these five grasping aggregates in four rounds, I announced my supreme perfect awakening in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans.
translation_text.sn22.56:2.1: And how are there four rounds?
translation_text.sn22.56:2.2: I directly knew form, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation.
translation_text.sn22.56:2.6: consciousness, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation.
translation_text.sn22.56:3.2: The four principal states, and form derived from the four principal states.
translation_text.sn22.56:3.6: The practice that leads to the cessation of form is simply this noble eightfold path, that is:
translation_text.sn22.56:3.7: right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
translation_text.sn22.56:4.1: Whatever ascetics and brahmins have directly known form in this way—and its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation—and are practicing for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding form: they are practicing well.
translation_text.sn22.56:4.2: Those who practice well have a footing in this teaching and training.
translation_text.sn22.56:5.1: Those ascetics and brahmins who have directly known form in this way—and its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation—and due to disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding form, are freed by not grasping: they are well freed.
translation_text.sn22.56:5.2: Those who are well freed are consummate ones.
translation_text.sn22.56:5.3: For consummate ones, there is no cycle of rebirths to be found.
translation_text.sn22.56:6.2: There are these six classes of feeling:
translation_text.sn22.56:6.3: feeling born of contact through the eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind.
translation_text.sn22.56:6.7: The practice that leads to the cessation of feelings is simply this noble eightfold path …
translation_text.sn22.56:9.2: There are these six classes of perception:
translation_text.sn22.56:9.3: perceptions of sights, sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and ideas.
translation_text.sn22.56:9.7: The practice that leads to the cessation of perceptions is simply this noble eightfold path …
translation_text.sn22.56:10.2: There are these six classes of intention:
translation_text.sn22.56:10.3: intention regarding sights, sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and ideas.
translation_text.sn22.56:10.7: The practice that leads to the cessation of choices is simply this noble eightfold path …
translation_text.sn22.56:13.2: There are these six classes of consciousness:
translation_text.sn22.56:13.3: eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind consciousness.
translation_text.sn22.56:13.5: Consciousness originates from name and form.
translation_text.sn22.56:13.6: When name and form cease, consciousness ceases.
translation_text.sn22.56:13.7: The practice that leads to the cessation of consciousness is simply this noble eightfold path, that is:
translation_text.sn22.56:13.8: right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
translation_text.sn22.56:14.1: Whatever ascetics and brahmins have directly known consciousness in this way—and its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation—and are practicing for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding consciousness: they are practicing well.
translation_text.sn22.56:14.2: Those who practice well have a footing in this teaching and training.
translation_text.sn22.56:15.1: Those ascetics and brahmins who have directly known consciousness in this way—and its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation—and due to disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding consciousness, are freed by not grasping: they are well freed.
translation_text.sn22.56:15.2: Those who are well freed are consummate ones.
translation_text.sn22.56:15.3: For consummate ones, there is no cycle of rebirths to be found.”
